courses.title: Engineering Mathematics II
courses.summary: Multivariable calculus, linear algebra, and second-order differential equations — the mathematics that vibrations, control systems, and heat transfer will spend the next three years consuming.
taught_from: MIT OCW 18.02 Multivariable Calculus; 18.03 Differential Equations (ocw.mit.edu)

lessons.scope: Dot and cross products with their physical readings: work and moment. Lines and planes in space. The geometry underneath 3-D statics revisited with better tools.
preview: What do the dot and cross products each measure physically — one plant example apiece?
Find the moment of a force about an axis: which product combination does it?

lessons.scope: Functions of several variables and their partial rates. The gradient as steepest ascent. Sensitivity thinking: which input moves the output most.
preview: What does the gradient's direction mean on a temperature field?
Which partial derivative answers 'which input moves the output most'?

lessons.scope: Multivariable chain rule and the total differential. Error propagation through a formula — the uncertainty calculation every measurement report owes. Linearization around an operating point.
preview: Propagate ±2% flow and ±1% head errors into pump power: which dominates and why?
What does linearization around an operating point throw away?

lessons.scope: Double and triple integrals over regions. Mass, centroid, and moment of inertia of solid parts computed properly. Order of integration chosen for sanity.
preview: Set up (don't solve) the triple integral for a stepped shaft's mass.
Why does integration order sometimes make an impossible integral easy?

lessons.scope: Vector fields as flow pictures. Line integrals and work; surface integrals and flux. The divergence and curl read physically — sources and swirl.
preview: Flux through a surface: what does a zero answer mean physically?
Read divergence and curl as statements about sources and swirl.

lessons.scope: Matrix algebra and Gaussian elimination. Solving real systems: truss equations, circuit equations, mass balances. Singularity as a physical warning, not just an algebraic one.
preview: What does a singular coefficient matrix signal about your truss model?
Why does Gaussian elimination beat matrix inversion for one solve?

lessons.scope: The eigenproblem stated and solved for small systems. Eigenvalues as natural rates and frequencies. The concept that becomes mode shapes in Vibrations.
preview: What physical quantity do eigenvalues become in vibrating systems?
Why do eigenvectors define directions the system 'prefers'?

lessons.scope: Homogeneous solutions: overdamped, critically damped, underdamped. The characteristic equation as the system's fingerprint. Mass-spring-damper solved completely — memorize this solution's shape.
preview: Classify overdamped, critical, underdamped by the characteristic roots.
Sketch the underdamped step response and label its envelope.

lessons.scope: Particular solutions for harmonic forcing. Amplitude and phase versus frequency; resonance and its danger. The mathematical skeleton of every vibration spectrum you will ever read.
preview: Why does response amplitude peak near the natural frequency?
What happens to phase as forcing sweeps through resonance?

lessons.scope: Transform pairs and properties; solving ODEs by algebra. Transfer functions previewed. The s-domain as the control engineer's home ground.
preview: Why does the Laplace transform turn an ODE into algebra?
What does a pole's location say about a response's shape?

lessons.scope: Periodic signals decomposed into harmonics. Spectra of square and sawtooth waves computed by hand. Why a vibration analyst's FFT screen shows lines where the machine has defects.
preview: Why does a square wave contain only odd harmonics?
Connect Fourier series to the lines on a vibration analyzer's screen.

courses.title: Engineering Dynamics
courses.summary: Motion with forces attached: kinematics of particles and rigid bodies, Newton–Euler methods, work–energy and impulse–momentum — aimed squarely at the rotating machinery this pathway lives on.

lessons.scope: Position, velocity, acceleration in rectilinear and curvilinear motion. Normal-tangential and polar descriptions. Reading motion from data rather than formulas.
preview: Distinguish normal and tangential acceleration on a curved conveyor path.
When is polar description more natural than Cartesian?

lessons.scope: F = ma applied with disciplined FBDs. Friction, drag, and constraint forces. Curvilinear applications: vehicles on curves, whirling masses.
preview: Why must the FBD precede F = ma, not follow it?
A mass on a curve at constant speed still accelerates: toward where, and why?

lessons.scope: Work of forces and couples; kinetic and potential energy. The work–energy theorem as an accounting identity. When energy methods beat force methods.
preview: When does the work–energy theorem beat force analysis?
Why does a couple's work involve angle, not distance?

lessons.scope: Linear impulse–momentum; conservation in collisions. Coefficient of restitution. Hammer blows and coupling impacts as plant-floor examples.
preview: What does the coefficient of restitution measure, and what are its limits 0 and 1?
Why do hammer blows deliver huge forces from modest energy?

lessons.scope: Center of mass motion and internal versus external forces. Steady mass flow forces — the jet on a plate that becomes pump and turbine thinking. Rocket-style variable-mass reasoning in brief.
preview: Why do internal forces cancel in a system of particles?
Compute the force of a steady water jet on a plate — which principle applies?

lessons.scope: Translation, fixed-axis rotation, and general plane motion. Relative velocity and instant centers. Gear and linkage motion analyzed cleanly.
preview: Locate the instant center of a rolling wheel — and use it to read velocities.
What makes general plane motion decomposable into translation plus rotation?

lessons.scope: Acceleration analysis of linkages. Rotating reference frames and the Coriolis term. Where slider-in-slot mechanisms get their surprising accelerations.
preview: Where does the Coriolis term appear in a slider-in-slot mechanism?
Why does acceleration analysis need velocity analysis first?

lessons.scope: Mass moment of inertia — measured, tabulated, and composed. ΣM = Iα for fixed-axis rotation and general plane motion. Starting torque of a loaded drum computed honestly.
preview: Why does mass moment of inertia depend on the axis chosen?
Starting torque of a loaded drum: which two terms add?

lessons.scope: Kinetic energy of rotation; angular impulse and momentum. Flywheels as energy stores — sizing one for load smoothing. Conservation demonstrations that survive into turbine trip analysis.
preview: Why does a flywheel smooth speed but not average torque?
Rotational KE: why does doubling speed quadruple stored energy?

lessons.scope: Static versus dynamic unbalance defined precisely. Single- and two-plane balancing logic. Why unbalance shows at 1× running speed — the vibration analyst's first spectral line explained by dynamics.
preview: Static versus dynamic unbalance: what does each do to bearing forces?
Why does pure static unbalance show at exactly 1× rotation frequency?

lessons.scope: The single degree-of-freedom oscillator from Newton's law. Natural frequency and damping ratio measured from a decay trace. The bridge lesson into the Year 3 Vibrations course.
preview: From a decay trace, how do you extract damping ratio?
What does natural frequency depend on — and pointedly not depend on?

courses.title: Materials Science II
courses.summary: The metallurgy that runs a steel-centric region: phase diagrams, the iron–carbon system, heat treatment, alloy and stainless steels, and the failure mechanisms — fatigue, creep, embrittlement — that maintenance exists to outrun.
taught_from: Callister & Rethwisch, Materials Science and Engineering: An Introduction (Wiley)

lessons.scope: Phases, components, and equilibrium. Binary isomorphous and eutectic systems; tie lines and the lever rule worked carefully. Reading a phase diagram as a map of what microstructure is possible.
preview: Read a eutectic diagram: what happens at the eutectic point on cooling?
Apply the lever rule: what does each lever arm represent?

lessons.t: The iron–carbon system and heat treatment
lessons.scope: Ferrite, austenite, cementite, pearlite; the eutectoid reaction. TTT/CCT thinking, quench-and-temper, hardenability, and the microstructures — martensite to tempered martensite — that set steel properties. The single most load-bearing lesson in this course.
preview: For 0.40 wt% C steel just below the eutectoid, use the lever rule: roughly what fraction is pearlite?
Quench austenite past the nose of the TTT curve: what forms, and why is tempering then non-negotiable?
Why does the core of a thick quenched shaft end up softer than its surface?

lessons.scope: Full anneal, normalize, stress relief, and spheroidize — what each buys and costs. Case hardening: carburizing, nitriding, induction. Choosing a treatment from a drawing callout.
preview: Full anneal versus normalize: what differs in cooling and in outcome?
Why does carburizing harden the case but not the core?

lessons.scope: Steel designation systems and what the digits encode. Effects of Mn, Cr, Mo, Ni on hardenability and toughness. Selecting among plain-carbon and low-alloy grades for shafts, gears, and structures.
preview: What does each digit of a 4140-style designation encode?
Which alloying elements buy hardenability, and what does that word mean?

lessons.scope: Austenitic, ferritic, martensitic, and duplex stainless families; sensitization as the classic trap. Gray, ductile, and white cast irons and their fracture signatures. Materials the Gulf's process plants order by the ton.
preview: Why does sensitization make welded austenitic stainless vulnerable at grain boundaries?
Gray versus ductile iron: what changes in graphite shape and in fracture?

lessons.scope: Aluminum and its temper designations; copper alloys — including the conductor grades drawn at Gulf Cable; nickel alloys for heat and corrosion. Where each family earns its cost premium.
preview: What does a T6 temper tell you happened to the aluminum?
Why is copper drawn at Gulf Cable annealed between passes?

lessons.scope: S–N behavior and endurance limits; fracture mechanics in its simplest honest form (K, toughness, flaw size); creep regimes and life estimation. The three mechanisms behind most rotating-equipment and high-temperature failures, with fracture-surface reading as the diagnostic skill.
preview: A polished steel has UTS 800 MPa: what first-cut endurance limit does the 0.5·UTS rule suggest, before knockdown factors?
Which two fracture-surface features distinguish fatigue from ductile overload?
How can a part pass its tensile test yet fail in service at half that stress?

lessons.scope: Thermoplastics versus thermosets; glass transition; viscoelasticity. Elastomers for seals — the O-ring as an engineering component. Temperature and chemical limits that kill polymer parts in service.
preview: What is the glass transition, and why does it matter for seals in Gulf heat?
Thermoplastic versus thermoset: which can be re-melted and why?

lessons.scope: Ceramic strength statistics and why tension is the enemy. Refractories — the furnace linings of a melt shop. Fiber composites and where they appear in industrial hardware.
preview: Why do ceramics fail statistically rather than at one strength?
What property makes refractories survive a melt shop?

lessons.scope: Oxidation and aqueous corrosion in outline; galvanic couples and passivity. Hydrogen embrittlement flagged for later. The trailer for Year 3's full corrosion course.
preview: Set up a galvanic couple: which metal corrodes, and what does area ratio do?
Why is passivity both stainless steel's shield and its trap?

lessons.scope: Property indices and screening; cost and availability as first-class constraints. Substitution decisions when the specified grade is unobtainable — a real procurement scenario. Documenting a selection so an auditor can follow it.
preview: Write a property index for a stiff, light beam — what ratio emerges?
A specified grade is unobtainable: what must a substitution memo prove?

courses.title: Thermodynamics I
courses.summary: Energy accounting with consequences: properties, the first law for closed systems and control volumes, the second law, and entropy — taught toward boilers, compressors, and turbines rather than abstract cycles.
taught_from: Çengel (with Boles), Thermodynamics: An Engineering Approach (McGraw-Hill) — via MIT OCW/NPTEL equivalents

lessons.scope: Closed and open systems; intensive and extensive properties; equilibrium and process paths. The state postulate in working form. Units and sign conventions fixed once, forever.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: Closed versus open system: give one plant example of each.
Why do sign conventions have to be fixed before any energy balance?

lessons.t: Pure substance properties and steam tables
lessons.scope: Phase regions, quality, and the P–v and T–s diagrams. Steam-table fluency drills — the skill utilities engineering assumes. Superheat and subcooling located physically.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: Read quality x = 0.85: what is physically in the vessel?
Locate superheat on a T–s diagram, and say why turbines want it.

lessons.scope: Boundary work computed for standard processes; heat as energy in transit, not a substance. Path dependence demonstrated. Reading work from a P–v diagram's area.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: Why is work path-dependent while internal energy is not?
Boundary work on a P–v diagram: what does the area under the curve mean?

lessons.scope: Energy balance for closed systems with internal energy and enthalpy distinguished. Specific heats and ideal-gas relations. Piston-cylinder and rigid-tank problems solved in a fixed format.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: When does enthalpy replace internal energy in a balance — and why?
Rigid tank versus piston-cylinder heated equally: which ends hotter, and why?

lessons.scope: Steady-flow energy equation term by term. Nozzles, turbines, compressors, pumps, throttles, and heat exchangers as one-line devices. The equation this pathway will use weekly for four years.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: Write the SFEE for a throttle: what survives and what dies?
Why does a turbine's Δh dwarf its ΔKE in almost every real case?

lessons.scope: Charging and discharging tanks; the uniform-state, uniform-flow model. Compressed-air receiver behavior as the working example. Where steady-flow assumptions break.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: Charging an air receiver: why does the tank end hotter than the supply line?
When does the steady-flow assumption honestly fail?

lessons.scope: Kelvin–Planck and Clausius statements; thermal efficiency and COP defined honestly. Carnot limits as ceilings no vendor brochure escapes. Refrigerators and heat pumps as reversed engines.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: Why can no engine beat Carnot between the same reservoirs?
COP above 1: why doesn't that violate anything?

lessons.scope: Entropy as a property; the increase principle for isolated systems. T–s diagrams and isentropic reference processes. Entropy generation as the bookkeeping of irreversibility.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: What does entropy generation measure that entropy change does not?
Why are isentropic processes the reference for real machines?

lessons.scope: Turbine, compressor, and pump efficiencies defined against the isentropic ideal. Real machine performance located on the h–s diagram. The number that separates a healthy compressor from a worn one.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: Isentropic efficiency 80% on a compressor: what does the extra work become?
Locate real versus ideal compression on an h–s diagram.

lessons.scope: Available work and dead states in outline. Exergy destruction as the cost of irreversibility. Why utilities audits speak this language — a preview of Thermo II.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: What is a dead state, and why does exergy need one?
Why is exergy destroyed while energy is merely converted?

lessons.scope: Otto, Diesel, and Brayton cycles in ideal form. Mean effective pressure and back-work ratio. Enough cycle literacy to hold a conversation about gas turbines — depth deferred to Thermo II.
lessons.src: NPTEL: Basic Thermodynamics; video: LearnChemE (CU Boulder)
preview: Why does the Brayton cycle's back-work ratio matter so much?
What does mean effective pressure summarize about a piston engine?

courses.title: Electrical Fundamentals for Mechanical Engineers
courses.summary: The electrical literacy a plant demands of its mechanical engineers: DC and AC circuits, three-phase power, transformers, and above all the induction motor — starter to nameplate to failure modes.

lessons.scope: The electrical quantities with water analogies used and then retired. Ohm's law and power dissipation. Reading a multimeter without folklore.
preview: Why does power dissipate as I²R, and what does that mean for loose connections?
What does a multimeter's input impedance protect against?

lessons.scope: Series-parallel reduction, Kirchhoff's laws, and voltage/current dividers. Practical sources and internal resistance. Battery-fed control circuits as examples.
preview: Two resistors in parallel: why is the result smaller than either?
What does Kirchhoff's voltage law conserve around a loop?

lessons.scope: Energy storage in fields; V–I relations for C and L. First-order transients and time constants. Why inductive loads arc switches and need suppression.
preview: Why does interrupting an inductive circuit draw an arc?
What does a time constant mean for a motor contactor's coil?

lessons.scope: Sinusoids, RMS values, and phasor representation. Impedance of R, L, C and series combinations. The 50 Hz world quantified.
preview: Why is RMS the honest rating for AC quantities?
What does impedance add to resistance, physically?

lessons.scope: Real, reactive, and apparent power; the power triangle. Power factor and why utilities bill for it. Correction capacitors sized in a worked example.
preview: A plant at power factor 0.7: what is flowing that does no work?
Size the correction idea: what do capacitors supply locally?

lessons.scope: Wye and delta connections; line versus phase quantities. Three-phase power calculation. Why industrial plants are three-phase places.
preview: Line versus phase voltage in a wye system: which ratio connects them?
Why is three-phase power delivery constant while single-phase pulses?

lessons.scope: Ideal transformer relations and real losses. Nameplate data decoded. Distribution transformers in a plant one-line diagram.
preview: What do the turns do in a transformer, and what stays (nearly) constant?
Read a transformer nameplate: which two numbers matter first?

lessons.scope: Rotating field, slip, and the torque–speed curve. Nameplate literacy: power, current, service factor, insulation class. The machine that drives nearly every pump and fan you will ever maintain.
preview: Define slip, and say why an induction motor cannot run at synchronous speed.
What does service factor 1.15 actually permit?

lessons.scope: The synchronous machine as motor and alternator: the rotating field met from the other side, synchronous speed, and excitation. Standby diesel generator sets — ratings, load acceptance, and the transfer logic that keeps critical plant alive. Paralleling conditions in outline: why voltage, frequency, and phase must agree before the breaker closes.
preview: Why does a synchronous machine run at exactly synchronous speed or not at all?
Name the three quantities that must match before paralleling a generator onto the bus.

lessons.scope: DOL and star-delta starting; overloads and protection classes. Variable-frequency drives in outline: what they do to the torque curve and to bearings. Motor circuit anatomy from MCC to terminal box.
preview: Why does star-delta starting cut inrush, and what does it cost in torque?
Name one thing a VFD does to bearings that DOL never did.

lessons.scope: Shock and arc-flash hazards respected properly; lockout-tagout as a mechanical engineer practices it. Insulation resistance and clamp-meter measurements. The boundary line between mechanical and electrical work scopes.
preview: What makes insulation resistance a trend, not a pass/fail number?
Where does the mechanical scope end at a motor terminal box — and why?

courses.title: Manufacturing Processes I — Casting & Bulk Forming
courses.summary: Liquid metal to near-net shape: the plant taxonomy, casting physics taught at full lecture depth, continuous casting, and the bulk deformation family — rolling, forging, extrusion, drawing — with Kuwait Steel and Gulf Cable as the standing examples.
taught_from: Groover, Fundamentals of Modern Manufacturing (Wiley) — casting & forming chapters
Kalpakjian & Schmid, Manufacturing Engineering and Technology (Pearson)

videos.title: SME — manufacturing process video library
videos.note: Short, clearly narrated explainers of casting, forming, and joining processes — the fastest visual companion to this course.

videos.title: 2.008 Design and Manufacturing II
videos.note: MIT's manufacturing-processes course: lecture notes, labs, and exams (no lecture videos in this edition).

videos.title: Fundamentals of Manufacturing Processes — IIT Roorkee
videos.note: Complete lecture-video series covering casting, forming, and joining at exactly this course's level.

videos.title: Manufacturing lectures by Eng. Mahmoud Marzouk
videos.note: Arabic-language manufacturing-processes lectures (casting, forming, sheet metal). English sources above remain the primary path.

lessons.t: The manufacturing plant: process taxonomy and material flow
lessons.scope: Every manufacturing operation sorted into a working taxonomy — casting, forming, machining, joining, finishing, assembly — and the material flow of a real plant traced end to end. The map the rest of the curriculum hangs on.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 1; Kalpakjian & Schmid, General Introduction
preview: Which two process families shape most of the world's metal by tonnage — and where does machining actually sit?
Classify every step of rebar production, scrap to bundled bar, into the six-family taxonomy.
Primary versus secondary shaping: define both with one example of each from a cable plant.

lessons.t: Metal casting I — solidification, gating, and riser design
lessons.scope: The full lecture: pouring energy balance, Bernoulli-derived gating design, Chvorinov's rule derived from mold heat conduction, shrinkage regimes, and riser sizing — with worked examples carried through in units.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 10; Flemings; Çengel & Ghajar, Ch. 4
preview: A casting has V/A = 2.0 cm and Cₘ = 2.5 min/cm²: how long until it is fully solid?
The free metal surface sits 250 mm above the sprue base: what is the base velocity by Torricelli?
Why must a riser out-modulus its casting, and what design margin did our worked example use?

lessons.t: Metal casting II — practice, quality, and defects
lessons.scope: From theory to the foundry floor: patterns and allowances, molding sand behavior, fluidity, and the defect families — gas porosity via Sievert's law, shrinkage porosity, hot tears, sand defects — each tied to its physical cause and its inspection method.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 10–11; Campbell, Complete Casting Handbook; ASM Handbook Vol. 15
preview: Halving the hydrogen partial pressure over a melt cuts dissolved hydrogen by what factor — and why not one-half?
Smooth spherical pores near the surface versus a jagged void in a thick boss: name each family and its matching fix.
A 9,000 cm³ steel casting shrinks 3% on solidification; the riser feeds 14% of its own volume. Minimum riser volume?

lessons.scope: The billet caster as a permanent-mold casting process running at steady state: shell growth, metallurgical length, breakout risk, and quality issues from rhomboidity to pinholes. Kuwait Steel's core process.
lessons.src: The Making, Shaping and Treating of Steel (AISE); Groover, Ch. 11
preview: A 140 mm square billet cast with K = 26 mm/min^½: minutes until the core is solid?
At 2.4 m/min casting speed, roughly how far below the mold must the torch cut — and why no closer?
Why is casting speed not a free productivity knob? Name both penalties.

lessons.t: Casting processes survey: sand to die casting
lessons.scope: Shell, investment, permanent mold, and die casting compared on tolerance, finish, section thickness, and economics. Expendable versus permanent molds as the organizing split. Choosing a casting route for a given part and volume.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 11; Kalpakjian & Schmid, casting chapters
preview: Rank sand, investment, and die casting by tolerance and by tooling cost — what pattern emerges?
Why does volume decide between expendable and permanent molds?

lessons.t: Fundamentals of metal forming and workability
lessons.scope: Flow stress and the flow curve; hot, warm, and cold working defined by homologous temperature. Strain-rate sensitivity in hot work, and friction and lubrication at the die interface — the tax every forming process pays. Workability limits and the defect families they guard — edge cracking, center bursts (chevrons), laps, and surface defects — read as process diagnostics, not random events.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 18–19; Kalpakjian & Schmid, forming fundamentals
preview: Define hot working by homologous temperature, and name its two big gifts.
Chevron cracks in a drawn bar: what process condition produces them?

lessons.scope: Flat and shape rolling from draft and bite condition through roll force and torque estimates. Hot rolling of billets into rebar as run at Kuwait Steel's mill. Rolled-product defects and their upstream causes.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 19; The Making, Shaping and Treating of Steel (AISE)
preview: With μ = 0.25 and 350 mm radius rolls, what is the maximum draft in a single pass?
Compute the contact length for Δh = 6 mm on 300 mm radius rolls.
Rolling needs the friction that other forming processes fight — why?

lessons.scope: Open-die and impression-die forging; upsetting analysis with friction; flash, draft, and forging loads. Grain flow as forging's signature advantage. What a forged flange or valve body buys over a casting.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 19; Kalpakjian & Schmid, forging chapter
preview: What true strain does upsetting from 80 mm to 50 mm impose?
For K = 600 MPa and n = 0.2 at ε = 0.47, what flow stress must the press overcome before friction?
What does grain flow buy in a forged crane hook — and in which loading direction?

lessons.scope: Direct and indirect extrusion; extrusion ratio and pressure estimates. Wire drawing through dies in tandem — reduction per pass, drawing stress, and why the wire must not yield in the drawn section. Gulf Cable's conductor line as the standing example.
lessons.src: Groover, Ch. 19; Kalpakjian & Schmid, extrusion & drawing chapters
preview: What is the extrusion ratio when a 100 mm billet becomes a 25 mm rod?
Why is a 60% area reduction impossible in one wire-drawing pass even with a perfect, frictionless die?
A pass gives draw stress 60 MPa on wire whose exit flow stress is 160 MPa: safe or failing, and by what criterion?

lessons.scope: Powder production, compaction, and sintering. Density as the controlling quality variable. Where PM wins: small complex parts at volume, porous bearings, and hard-to-melt materials.
lessons.src: Groover, powder metallurgy chapter; Kalpakjian & Schmid
preview: Why is density the master quality variable of powder metallurgy?
Where does PM beat machining economically, and why?

lessons.scope: Choosing among casting, forming, and machining for a given geometry, alloy, tolerance, and volume. Fixed versus variable cost and the break-even volume between processes. Near-net-shape thinking as an economic argument.
lessons.src: Groover, process selection discussions; Kalpakjian & Schmid
preview: Set up the break-even volume between a die-cast and a machined part.
What does near-net-shape actually save — material, energy, or machining time?
